React Basics: Getting to Know useEffect (and Side Effects) — (8)
Next up we're getting to know a new React Hook:
. The name looks a little odd at first glance, because if you translate it straight into plain English it's basically "use effect." Effect of what, exactly? Let's find out.
, there's one concept we need to nail down first: the Side Effect. So what's a Side Effect?
The classic analogy is the "medicine" a doctor prescribes.

Yep, exactly the kind you're thinking of — the stuff on the prescription. Picture it: after you catch a cold, the bag of meds you get usually has "side effects" printed on it somewhere, and those side effects might be things like an allergic reaction, drowsiness, or an upset stomach.
I'll admit the word "side effect" makes everything sound bad, but a side effect can actually be helpful and beneficial too. Here's a quick way to look at it:
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
After you take the medicine your body gets better, but along with it comes an upset stomach
That little description actually contains two side effects: "your body gets better" and "an upset stomach." So side effects aren't all bad.
So why am I bringing this up? Well, the things we run into all the time — AJAX, DOM manipulation, even
— are all a kind of side effect. And in real development we lean on side effects constantly, so understanding them helps you avoid writing some dangerous code.
So in the world of code, what does a side effect look like? For example, reassigning a value is also a side effect:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Bet you didn't expect that reassigning a value counts as a side effect.
But it's true — anytime you mutate the original value, that's a side effect. You can see the original value above is
, and then who-knows-how-many hundreds or thousands of lines later we reassign
. That whole process can lead to unpredictable bugs that are a pain to track down.
This is just a rough overview, of course. If this area interests you, check out something I wrote earlier — "
A Fun, Simple Take on Functional Programming in JavaScript
I'm only touching on what a side effect is here, since the React docs put it like this:
Data fetching, setting up a subscription, and manually changing the DOM in React components are all examples of side effects.
Alright, now that we've got a rough idea of what a side effect is, let's get to know

— things like the AJAX calls and DOM changes I mentioned earlier.

Time to meet a new React Hook: useEffect. The name looks a little odd at first — "use effect"? Effect of what? In this post we'll first get a handle on what a Side Effect actually is, then walk through every flavor of useEffect: running after every render, running once, watching specific values, and the cleanup function. I'll also compare each one to its Vue equivalent so it clicks faster.
